channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Inside the Ebola Epicenter, the Virus Rages With Little to Stop It
item.description: A remote gold mining town is under siege, as medical workers struggle to beat back a surge of deaths and infections.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: What to Know About the Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Aid agencies are racing to help underequipped health workers in the Democratic Republic of Congo. More than 245 people are now suspected to have died from the virus.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg, Ephrat Livni and Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Disinfecting a path outside a hospital in Mongbwalu, a mining town at the center of the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

item.title: Judge Reopens Trump’s Lawsuit Demanding $10 Billion From IRS
item.description: The ruling was a blow to both President Trump, who had voluntarily dismissed the suit last week, and to the Justice Department, which used the suit to establish a fund likely intended for Trump allies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: In a stern order on Friday, a judge said that she wanted to investigate the circumstances surrounding President Trump’s efforts to settle the lawsuit in a way that benefited him and his allies.

item.title: Kennedy Center Must Remove Trump’s Name From Building, Judge Orders
item.description: In an incensed social media post, President Trump suggested that the ruling might prompt him to cast the center aside after more than a year at its helm.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Zach Montague
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Congress gave the Kennedy Center its name, and only Congress can change it,” Judge Christopher R. Cooper of the Federal District Court in Washington wrote in a ruling on Friday.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From a Kennedy Center Ruling That Angered Trump
item.description: A federal judge ordered the Kennedy Center to take President Trump’s name off the building. What happens next?
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, the chairman of the Kennedy Center, wrote on social media that “we are going to be working with Congress to transfer this failing Institution back to them.”

item.title: Is JD Vance the 2028 Front Runner? Trump Has Questions.
item.description: President Trump appears to see the matter of his heir as unsettled, adding a layer of tension to his relationship with Vice President JD Vance.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times

item.title: Powerful A.I. Super PACs Duel Over the Midterms: ‘This Is a War’
item.description: One super PAC is allied with Anthropic. The other is tied to OpenAI. They’re both spending millions to influence this year’s elections.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The artificial intelligence industry has begun spending tens of millions of dollars on elections, routing the money through allied super PACs.

item.title: Steyer Campaigns on Affordability. Does His Own Mansion Portfolio Matter?
item.description: Tom Steyer wants to address California’s housing crisis if he becomes governor. He is also a billionaire with extensive personal real estate holdings.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight and Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Rachel Bujalski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tom Steyer has campaigned as a progressive Democrat who wants to make housing affordable in the state. His own wealth has been a concern for some voters.

item.title: A Candidate’s Past Ties to a Militant Cleric Are Surfacing in a N.J. House Race
item.description: Dr. Adam Hamawy, a New Jersey Democrat with big-name support on the left, has touted his humanitarian work, but some opponents want to talk about Sheikh Omar Abdel Rahman.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times

item.title: Iran’s Hard-Liners Try to Derail Potential Deal With the U.S.
item.description: A political fight is playing out in Iran, where the small but loud faction of hard-liners has used rallies, state media and private and public statements to try to undermine negotiations.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard of the late supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and his successor, Mojtaba Khamenei, during a ceremony in Tehran last Sunday.

item.title: As Trump Mulls Decision About Iran War Deal, a Restive Middle East Waits to Hear
item.description: The president has wavered on whether to move ahead with an agreement with Iran to end the war. On Friday, he vowed to make a “final determination” soon.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said on Friday that he was meeting with advisers to make a “final determination” about a proposed deal with Iran.

item.title: Across the Middle East, Muslims Mark Eid Amid War and Crisis
item.description: From Iran to Gaza, Eid al-Adha celebrations were muted as war dragged on and shortages of food and fuel roiled the region.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Bilal Shbair
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Palestinians prayed near the ruins of Al-Huda Mosque in Khan Younis, in southern Gaza, on Wednesday, the first day of Eid al-Adha. Israel bombed the mosque early in the war in Gaza.

item.title: The Russian Drone That Hit Romania Also Hit European Confidence
item.description: The failure to protect NATO territory further increased anxiety about alliance solidarity, Russia’s intentions and Washington’s commitment to collective defense.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Residents near a building that was struck by Russian drone in Galati, eastern Romania, on Friday.

item.title: A Big Bang, a Fire and Panic as War Enters Romanian Homes
item.description: When a drone crashed into an apartment building in eastern Romania, residents were reminded that the Russia-Ukraine war makes for a dangerous neighbor.
item.dc:creator: Andreea Campeanu and Andrew Higgins
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times
item.media:description: A temporary cover for a hole in the roof of a building in Galati, Romania, on Saturday, after a Russian drone slammed into a residential block on Friday.

item.title: How Ukraine’s Street Style Is Shaped by the War
item.description: In Kyiv, military-inspired fashion can be seen everywhere. Soldiers and civilians alike say it’s a show of solidarity, not a fad.
item.dc:creator: Daria Mitiuk and Brendan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times

item.title: Want to ‘Optimize’ Your Happiness? This Happiness Expert Says: Don’t.
item.description: Laurie Santos on what will really bring meaning and fulfillment to your life, and what won’t.
item.dc:creator: Lulu Garcia-Navarro
item.media:credit: Philip Montgomery for The New York Times

item.title: Making Motherhood and a Long Tennis Career Possible
item.description: Tennis is a solitary sport, but with new fertility benefits, a sisterhood of egg freezers is emerging in locker rooms and players’ lounges.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
item.media:credit: Matthew Avignone for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I have found this absolutely newfound admiration for women that go through those cycles over and over,” Magda Linette said.

item.title: The First A.I. High School in the U.S. Is Surprisingly Human
item.description: Schools are not like start-ups, because children’s minds should not be tied to the whims of the marketplace.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Grose and Jill Frank
item.media:credit: Jill Frank for The New York Times

item.title: The Best News in America
item.description: A low murder rate is by no means the only measure of a good society. But it’s a pretty good measure of a society’s underlying stability.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times

item.title: Season Pass
item.description: Today, let’s set our ambitions for summer reading.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Among Mamdani’s Priorities, Economic Development Seems Low on the List
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani has not filled the vacant role of president of the Economic Development Corporation, deepening concern over his attention to the New York City economy.
item.dc:creator: Dana Rubinstein, Sally Goldenberg, Jeffery C. Mays and Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s team has considered at least 10 people as they look to hire a president of the city’s Economic Development Corporation.

item.title: Colombia’s Elections Are a Crucial Test for the Left in Latin America
item.description: Colombia’s leftist candidate for president leads in polls, but his main rival — a far-right outsider who calls himself “The Tiger” — has gained momentum. Elections are Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo De La Espriella, a far-right candidate for president of Colombia, closed his campaign in the city of Medellín.

item.title: In a Quiet Corner of America, Greyhound Racing Hangs On. For Now.
item.description: West Virginia is home to the country’s last greyhound races, a sport that once drew millions of spectators each year but may soon be banned nationwide.
item.dc:creator: Stefano Montali and Rebecca Kiger
item.media:description: The greyhound track at Wheeling Island Hotel, Casino & Racetrack in Wheeling, W.Va.

item.title: How to Protect 529 College Savings in Case of Divorce or Death
item.description: Many couples don’t consider 529 accounts carefully when splitting up, and other families make a crucial mistake that can leave a mess if somebody dies.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber
item.media:credit: Robert Neubecker
